subject: PhD Numerical Analysis
instructions: Do 4 (four) of the first 5 (1–5) and 4 (four) of the last 5 problems (6–10).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: The matrix 2-norm is invariant under unitary transformation.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: If \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) is diagonal, then for any matrix \(p\)-norm \(\|\cdot\|_p\), it holds that \(\|A\|_p = \max_{1\leq i\leq n}|a_{ii}|\).

problems.text: Suppose \(A\in\mathbb{C}^{m\times n}\). Prove or provide a counterexample for each of the following.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: The Frobenius norm of a matrix is induced by (subordinate to) a vector norm.

problems.text: Let \(A\in\mathbb{C}^{n\times n}\). You may use the fact (if you find it useful) That \(A\) has a Schur decomposition. Prove or provide a counterexample for each of the following.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: If \(A\) is both normal and triangular then it is diagonal.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: If \(A\) is normal then the 2-norm of \(A\) is equal to it’s spectral radius \(\rho(A)\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Let \(r_i=\sum_{j=1,j\ne i}^n|a_{ij}|\). Let \(D_i\) be the disk in \(\mathbb{C}\) with center \(a_{ii}\) and radius \(r_i\). Prove that if \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\), then \(\lambda\in\bigcup_i D_i\); in other words, \(\lambda\) is in at least one of the disks \(D_i\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Determine all eigenvalues of the Householder reflector \(H(w)\), including their multiplicities. Justify your answer.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Find a full singular value decomposition of \(A\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Find an economy QR decomposition of \(B\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that \(g\) is a contraction on \(G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(x_{k+1}=g(x_k)\) converges to an unique fixed point \(z\in G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\) for any initial value \(x_0\in G=[\ln 1.1,\ln 3]\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Derive this formula by using Taylor’s theorem.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Derive this formula by using Lagrange polynomial representation.

problems.text:
Given a differentiable function \(f(x)\), consider the problem of finding a polynomial \(p(x)\in\mathbb{P}^n\) such that
\[
p(x_0)=f(x_0),\qquad p'(x_i)=f'(x_i),\qquad i=1,2,\ldots,n,
\]
where \(x_i\), \(i=1,2,\ldots,n\), are distinct nodes. (It is not excluded that \(x_1=x_0\).) Show that the problem has a unique solution and describe how it can be obtained.

Let \(Q_m(f)\) denote the \(m\)-point Gaussian quadrature rule over the interval \([a,b]\) and with continuous weight function \(\rho(x)\geq 0\), that is,
\[
Q_m(f)=\sum_{i=1}^m\alpha_i f(x_i)\approx J(f)=\int_a^b\rho(x)f(x)\,dx.
\]
Show that, if \(a\) and \(b\) are finite and \(f\) is continuous, then \(Q_m(f)\to J(f)\) as \(m\to\infty\).

Consider numerically solving the initial value problem
\[
y'(t)=f(t,y),\quad 0<t\leq t_f,\qquad\text{with }y(0)=\eta.
\]
Assume \(f\) is sufficiently differentiable and let \(h\) denote the step size. Show that all convergent members of the family of methods
\[
y_{n+2}+(\theta-2)y_{n+1}+(1-\theta)y_n=\frac14h\bigl[(6+\theta)f_{n+2}+3(\theta-2)f_n\bigr],
\]
parameterized by \(\theta\), are also \(A_0\)-stable.
